content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies that switch genres or dramatically change for the remainder of the movie?

**Score:** 46
**Body:** I am looking for movies that start as one type of thing and then completely change into something else entirely. I am mainly looking for genre switches, but anything that changes the movie in a major final way. The first movie that comes to mind is Full Metal Jacket, where its more of comedy until it turns into a war movie. I plan on picking a suggestion blindly. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Cabin in the Woods
2. First one that comes to mind is the Korean movie parasite (2019).
3. From Dusk til dawn (and Sinners)
Sorry to bother you 
  - from dusk till dawn is the gold standard for this. first half is a straight up tense crime thriller, then it just becomes a completely different movie out of nowhere. And i would also add that sinners is more of the gangster and country tone side of that, almost like godfather meets vampire from brooklyn lol 
4. THE WORLDS END with Simon Pegg and Nick Frost. Starts as a “old friends get together” and does an abrupt left turn into something else.
5. Radioflash (2019)

Barbarian (2022)
6. Adaptation 
7. Triangle of sadness (2022)
8. Crying Game 
9. Bone Tomahawk

## 2. I’m in the mood for erotic forbidden love movie that’s full of tension

**Score:** 32
**Body:** I’m in the mood for a deeply romantic yet intense film that revolves around a forbidden love—perhaps an affair or an unexpected age gap—where every interaction is charged with both emotional and physical tension. I want something that doesn’t hold back on passion, where the connection between the characters feels almost overwhelming, pulling them toward each other despite the consequences. The story should linger on stolen glances, lingering touches, and moments of quiet vulnerability, building a sense of sensual intimacy that’s just as powerful emotionally as it is physically. I’m drawn to narratives where desire and conflict intertwine, where love feels dangerous yet irresistible, and where the atmosphere is thick with longing, secrecy, and raw, unspoken emotion. I’ll appreciate recommendations thanks!!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. portrait of a lady on fire 
2. The Age of Innocence (1993)

In the Mood for Love (2000)
  - Definitely In The Mood For Love. Such a great movie
3. THE HANDMAIDEN
4. Lust, Caution is pretty good.
5. Brokeback Mountain
6. Dreamers (2003) might fit your request. 
7. ‘Guinivere’ (1994) has plenty of taboo elements in it. 

‘The Sessions’ (2012) is about a sex therapist and a quadraplegic patient who have a curious relationship. It’s quite beautiful. 

‘Call Me By Your Name’ is one of the more recent films with a love story that fits the bill.
8. Secretary with James Spader and Maggie Gyllenhaal. 
9. Kama Sutra
Like water for chocolate 

## 3. Movies like Kingsman?

**Score:** 28
**Body:** I've just watched Kingsman: The Secret Service and I loved every single second of it. I don't normally go for spy type movies but a few YouTube Shorts had me very curious.

The fast pace, the coolness, the humour, amazing soundtrack, hugely satisfying and loveable characters, it ticked everything for me and I haven't enjoyed a movie so much in a long time tbh.

Doesn't even have to be a spy movie, but can anyone recommend anything remotely as exhilarating and fantastic please?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Gentlemen (2019), by Guy Ritchie. 

Its not the same genre but pretty similar in style. 
2. The Ministry of Ungentlemanly Warfare
3. Kick-Ass 

Bullet Train 

The Man from U.N.C.L.E.
4. Bullet train 
5. Wanted (2008), Smokin' Aces (2006), American Ultra (2015), The Man from U.N.C.L.E. (2015), The Old Guard (2020), RRR (2022)
6. Our Man Flint (1966) Action/Comedy There were a lot of James Bond parodies in the 60s. This was one of the best. 
7. Maybe not *just* like *Kingsman* but I'd recommend any of the following to someone who enjoyed it:

 Atomic Blonde

Hardcore Henry 

Boy Kills World

Guns Akimbo

Boss Level

Mayhem

Hotel Artemis
8. Nobody and Nobody 2
9. When I first saw Kingsman in theaters I knew it was a gem. Such a damn good movie, too bad it didn't take off, it could have been a nice addition to the Bond/Mission Impossible type of flick.

Also I think Samuel Jackson was underrated in his role. This man is old as hell yet he really brought a Millennial energy to his character. He just had a really fresh approach and it was funny to see him so seamlessly play that character like he did.
10. Bit less hoppity flashy, but you might like Spy Game (2001). 

  
For more of the same tone, i'd say Kick-Ass (2010). 

## 4. Help I need a movie like the 40 year old virgin

**Score:** 11
**Body:** It doesn’t necessarily have to be comedy. Just some guy who struggles with dating and social life. I would really like some similar movies where the main characters have these problems, as it’s something I relate to.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Omg. Lars and the Real Girl
2. Her (2013). Heads up if you haven’t seen it, it’s very bleak and pretty much the opposite of 49 yr old virgin 

Edit: 40 lol
3. Punch-Drunk Love in a weird way… not the same but similar energy
4. Crazy Stupid Love

Swingers
5. Grandma’s boy
6. Hitch
7. Not a movie but 'Hello Ladies" is a pretty funny series on HBO.
8. Dan in Real Life  , a nice movie with Steve Carell
9. It's set in high school, but The Perks of Being a Wallflower covers this. 

What's cool is that the author of the book (which is so good) directed the movie, so while there were changes made, there wasn't a huge disconnect like you sometimes get.
10. secret life of Walter mitty

## 5. Requesting anything with the vibe of "Bernie" & "Lars and the Real Girl"

**Score:** 12
**Body:** Recently I realized I've got a thing for movies where the plot can be loosely described as "Stories about a kind weirdo and the town that loves them." As stated by the title, I'd put both Bernie and Lars and the Real Girl up there as peak examples of the vibe I want more of. I did some googling around, one of the others I've seen before that definitely fits is Edward Scissorhands; I'm not usually a Tim Burton or Johnny Depp fan, but that one nails the genre so much I love it anyway. 

  
I'm curious if reddit has anything along these lines. I've got a shortlist to add to the watchlist from google, but it's always nice to get a little discussion going and get ideas from real folks :) 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Swiss Army Man

Ride the Eagle
2. Maybe Little Miss Sunshine?
3. Lucas (1986)
4. Honestly I think the muppets movie (2011) fits this critera. If you haven’t seen the Truman Show I recommend that.
5. A Man Called Otto

The Terminal
6. It has been a while, but Butter might be good. 
7. The Station Agent
8. Brian & Charles (2022)


Just go in blind. Trust me.
9. *Stuck on You* by the Farrelly Brothers. They leave the town a bit of the way through but totally fits. Matt Damon and Greg Kinnear play conjoined twins and like the Farrelly Brothers typically do, they handle characters with disabilities in what I've always found to be a pretty loving way.

Similarly, *Mr. Deeds* with Adam Sandler when he's in his small town. Not the greatest movie but I liked it when I was young and watched it recently and didn't find anything to hate about it.

Also *Hot Rod* with Andy Samberg. Everyone in town seems to be supportive of his antics.
10. Drop Dead Gorgeous and Drowning Mona ( the second one has a not likeable character but also a bunch of good small town characters)

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Name a villain that got the most horrific death

**Score:** 1990
**Body:** Emi Antonowsky from Robocop got toxic waste all over and he’s in pain since his entire skin was melting, and he got run over by his boss and his entire body exploded.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Mama from dredd would be a crappy way to go. Slow mo all the way to the bottom. 
  - Judgment time

![gif](giphy|10MlVunnRFnQ2Y)
2. Nicky Santoro in Casino.
3. Dude whose face melted off in Raidets of the Lost Ark
  - If I had a penny for every Nazi guy dressed in black wearing round glasses.
4. The dude from Looper who slowly lost his limbs was horrifying.
5. Hugh Jackman's character in The Prestige dies hundreds of times drowning in a tube. 
6. He was only a side villain at best, but Viserys in *Game of Thrones* getting a "golden crown" was brutal. 

![gif](giphy|YFn46DerlaOeQ)


  - Martin was no doubt inspired by the real life event of Crassus, once Rome’s richest man, rumored to have been killed by having molten gold poured down his throat
7. ![gif](giphy|x9wOCLZEsDNRhvJEkb)

Such a good scene. Needed more of this.

## 2. Anyone else remember Henry Cavill's role in the Mission: Impossible franchise?

**Score:** 1063
**Body:** This in MIssion: Impossible - Fallout. What do you think of his portrayal here so far? I personally liked it and he did well IMO as an antagonist. I'm shocked that IRL, he's 21 years younger than Tom Cruise.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Do I remember him reloading his arms? Yes, yes I do remember him reloading his arms.

https://i.redd.it/whua7qxfoztg1.gif
  - There is a great story about this gesture. It was inadvertent. Cavill was doing it between takes because his muscles were tensing up. Director loved the look of it and told him to add it on camera. 

https://youtube.com/shorts/XayhmNKWVpM?si=dWFBSJr9wopzTpcM
    - I thought he did it to adjust his shirt sleeves lol
      - No it was to regrow his beard. Watch the clip again lol
        - Omggg... I need to tell my brother that *this* is the trick he's been looking for! 
2. The world’s most expensive moustache.
3. Do I remember trying to grow a Cavillian moustache to impress my enamoured wife and failing miserably? Yes. 
4. After philip seymour hoffman in the third one, Cavill was the second best villain in the franchise
  - In third place? The mustache.
5. Arguably the best film in the franchise. Everyone remembers 

## 3. I finally watched Michael Mann's Heat (1995)...

**Score:** 989
**Body:** ...and it's AN ABSOLUTE BANGER. My only regret is not watching it sooner

Holy hell, there's so much. To start, the shootout scenes were impeccable pieces of live wire filmmaking, the performers were all in top form. It's one of those movies where you suddenly realize a bunch of other films have ripped it off lol I really like the two opposing forces that respect each other as they violently intertwine in terms of plot

Have absolutely no clue how they're making a prequel or sequel to this lol at least Michael Mann is doing it. The first film by him I saw was as a kid, which was Public Enemies and I think that's actually kind of underrated

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. For me the action is the juice
  - ![gif](giphy|iXooEoi1FTee6w8FP9)

For me it's...
2. I will die on a hill that Al Pachino ignored the script and just played himself.
  - ![gif](giphy|9oLcjSWCpi51OQaroW)
  - The original plan was to show that Hanna was a coke head but it was cut. Pacino’s performance makes much more sense when you consider that. 
3. Loser brag, I was an extra in the one on one De Niro/ Pacino scene. 
  - I would never shut the fuck up about that lol
4. I watched for the first time the other day and I thought the same thing! Excellent movie!!
  - ![gif](giphy|9EvnXdZaUZbCqScn67)
5. ![gif](giphy|pQLmrZN8r1p0Q)

## 4. Interstellar is a solid 9.5 out of 10 but I think it’s fair to say that without Hans Zimmer’s unbelievable soundtrack it would probably be an 8.

**Score:** 729

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I 100% agree with this statement.  I was having this exact conversation with my wife and kids the other day (my kids love the soundtrack), we all agreed that while it's amazing movie.  It wouldn't be nearly as enjoyable without Hans Zimmer. 
  - Yeah the movie is genuinely amazing but Hans Zimmers soundtrack is SO good that I just can’t imagine it being as good with anything different. 
2. Yes and this can be applied to a lot of great films.

Imagine

The Spaghetti trilogy and Once Upon a Time in the West without Morricone.

Jaws, Star Wars, Indiana Jones, Superman without Williams.

Blade Runner, Chariots of Fire, 1492 without Vangelis.

Twin Peaks without Badalementi.

Etc

Films that are great but would be nowhere as epic if it wasn’t for the masterful scores.





  - 💯 and some great examples there. Honourable mentions too for Gladiator, Braveheart, Titanic and Last of the Mohicans 
  - Don't forget Inception, will be boring as hell without Zimmer
3. The "Docking scene" music is absolute cinema
4. Went to see it at the Royal Albert Hall last weekend with an orchestra!!!! 

Amazing! 

Docking scene! 🤌
5. I really liked it. Then I watched it on shrooms and it transcended into greatness for me 
6. Well yes, but the vast majority of films would have the same issue
7. Hans Zimmer went to GOD mode in this movie.....I almost listen to the soundtrack as a focus music every week.

## 5. Would you have preferred to see Sacha Baron Cohen's version Bohemian Rhapsody?

**Score:** 601
**Body:** It’s honestly one of those “what could have been” situations. Sacha Baron Cohen as Freddie Mercury in a full on, no holds barred biopic just sounds way more interesting than what we ended up getting lol.

From everything that’s come out, Cohen wanted to go all in, not just the music and the iconic moments, but the messy, complicated reality of Freddie’s life. The identity struggles, the insane party lifestyle, the drug use, the loneliness, and eventually the AIDS diagnosis. Basically, a proper character study instead of a highlight reel.

And the thing is, Cohen absolutely has the acting chops to pull that off. People forget how good he can be in serious roles. I genuinely think he could’ve delivered something really layered and uncomfortable in a good way.

But then you’ve got Brian May and Roger Taylor wanting a more “celebration of Queen” type film, something more accessible and PG leaning. Which fair enough, it’s their legacy at the end of the day.

And yeah, *Bohemian Rhapsody* made a shit ton of money and introduced Queen to a whole new audience, so from that perspective it clearly worked. But man, it just feels like we missed out on a much more raw, honest, and probably more memorable film.

Idk, I get why they went the safe route, but I definitely would’ve preferred Cohen’s darker version. Feels like it would’ve had way more to say instead of just playing the hits.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 100% yes. Rami was good. But Sacha would have  been better. And the movie would have been darker and more personal. I know Sacha wanted to lean more into Freddy's personal life. But the band wanted to keep it more about the band. Which makes sense
  - According to movie Brian May never had any issues, with drugs, alcohol, ego, or women. It's pretty amazing and I'm sure it has nothing to do with the fact that he was a producer of the movie.
    - Also the fact that he required an equal amount of screen time between all four of them, so the movie constantly jump cuts like a Transformers movie.
      - And won an Oscar for editing! 
    - That’s honestly one of the things that really bugs me about that movie too. Like, they basically turned the other three guys into saints, no drinking, no drugs, just pure, disciplined angels the whole time. Really?? Come on, lmao. It feels so unrealistic, especially when you know what rock bands were actually like back then.

And the funny part is, in real life, Brian May wasn’t exactly some squeaky clean choirboy either. He definitely had his wild side and could party with the rest of them. But for some reason, the film just completely glosses over that and paints him and the others as if they were totally above all that. It ends up making Freddie look like the only one going off the rails, which just isn’t a fair or accurate portrayal of the band dynamic at all.

It’s like they simplified everything to make it cleaner and more dramatic, but in doing that, they kind of stripped away the real complexity of who they all were.
      - Just rewatched it the other night actually, and yeah they even have a scene where they’re sitting next to their wives or long term girlfriends in the middle of Freddie’s house during a party before they politely get up and excuse themselves.

It is genuinely bizarre seeing how far they go to depict the other bandmates as essentially perfect - the only argument they ever really show them having is a polite disagreement about whether “I’m in Love with My Car” should be included on the album
    - Remember when we learned from Straight Outta Compton that Dr. Dre and Ice Cube were just super cool guys that never did anything uncool, particularly towards women, and their disagreements with Eazy E were really just Paul Giammati’s fault and they were actually pretty much back to being best friends right before he died?

Wait, who produced this movie?
2. Midgets with trays on cocaine taped to their heads for sure 
3. I just don't think Malek is a leading man.
4.   The biography was dishonest, added fake drama that didn't happen while not including most of the real drama surrounding the band during that time.  it was basically a sanitized "VH1 meet the band" documentary used as a vehicle to sell more albums.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Terrence Malick’s women are beautiful but barely people

**Score:** 124
**Body:** I feel like I have a weirdly specific gripe with Terrence Malick’s newer films, and I’m curious if anyone else feels this or if I’m just being picky.

I don’t hate his movies. They’re beautiful, and I get why people love them. But the way he portrays women has always felt a little off to me.

Like in The Tree of Life, the mom is one of the only times I’ve felt like a Malick woman actually has an inner life. You can feel her grief, her frustration, that quiet spiritual struggle. She feels real.

But at the same time, she’s also kind of a walking perfume ad.

Everything about her is so aestheticized. The spinning, the sunlight, the floating, the whispering. Even when she’s struggling, it’s presented in this soft, almost sacred, weightless way, and it kind of undercuts it for me.

I get that the movie is from Jack’s perspective, so it makes sense she’d feel mythologized, like this symbol of grace or comfort. And honestly, that works there. It feels intentional.

But I don’t think that fully explains it, because he does this with women in a lot of his films. Like in To the Wonder, it’s the same thing but without that clear framing, and I just couldn’t buy into it.

It feels like he strips away anything grounded and replaces it with pure feeling. His female characters don’t really make clear choices or express specific wants. You don’t get much sense of their agency. They just kind of drift around, touching things, spinning, whispering, and mostly existing in relation to the man’s inner life.

It’s like they become symbols, “love,” “grace,” “longing,” instead of actual people who are messy and contradictory.

And the camera reinforces that. It’s always circling them, catching the light, making them feel almost untouchable. So even when they’re there, they don’t feel fully present.

What’s frustrating is you can see moments where they almost feel real, and then the movie pulls them right back into that floaty, idealized version.

So I end up kind of split. Sometimes it works, like in Tree of Life, but a lot of the time it just feels like he’s smoothing women down into something prettier and less human.

Idk. Maybe I’m overthinking it, or maybe he’s got some unresolved mom stuff lol.

Curious if anyone else feels this way.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Same thing with the men. Affleck is a complete non-human being in "To the Wonder." Malick is just an abstract, impressionistic filmmaker who isn't that concerned with people, he is concerned with religion, philosophy, and nature. 
  - And his scripts are very different from the films. I remember Penn commenting that *The Tree of Life* was the most magnificent script he'd ever read or probably would ever read, and he was disappointed in the film that ultimately resulted, because it was so different.

When I later read a draft of the *Days of Heaven* script (and I love that film), I knew what Penn was talking about. Malick's descriptions, stage directions, and sometimes dialogue that doesn't make it into the movie suggest he does understand people well. But what he prepares fully in the writing only comes through in fragments in the movie.

Abby/Brooke Adams's little story of how when she was Linda's age, she used to work rolling cigars and she never saw sunlight, and "This \[their present situation\] is not so bad," is a good example. In the script, that's one of many details establishing who Abby is and where she's come from. In the film, she's more enigmatic.
  - I feel in most of his films the men are flawed characters who externalize their struggles. Like the dad in tree of life. He is a messy human who alternates between questioning himself and following his nature. We don’t get as many moments like that for the women of his films. 
    - Have you seen badlands? The woman is the ONLY “real person” in that film
2. This seems to be a criticism of just Tree of Life, and perhaps some of his male-focused films like Thin Red Line (understandable as it is a world war 2 film) or Too the Wonder/Knight of Cups (those can be seen as autobiographical extensions of Tree of Life). However it would be a strange criticism of all his other films. 

Badlands, A Hidden Life, Song to Song, Days of Heaven, and The New World are fully or shared perspective of female characters. Frazisca’s struggles and acceptance of her husband are key to a Hidden Life. The impacts and struggles of Pocahontas are front and centre in A New World. I could go on. Would you argue the female characters are just symbols in these films???

Also for Tree of Life, of course the mom is symbolic. So is the dad in his own way. The film is from a child’s perspective and is primarily focussed on the way of nature and the way of grace with each parent symbolizing one of those ways through life. 

And with The Thin Red Line this is perfectly reasonable as we are embedded in the war. Do you have the same criticisms of other world war 2 films? Do you decry the lack of female characters in Saving Private Ryan or Das Boot?
3. I feel like you're expanding your feelings about The Tree of Life into his wider body of work, but this flattens his other work somewhat. I'll pick two examples. Badlands is fully a two-hander between two characters who are equivalently well-developed, and moreover Holly (Spacek) is our narrator and entry-point into the story. Her character is given just as much shading as Kit, and we're privy to her musings and inner life throughout the film in a way that we aren't with him. I'd also point to The New World's portrayal of Pocahontas/Rebecca as one of his most nuanced depictions of any character: we're subtly let in on all her complexity as a person in a way that frustrates the expectations of the (mostly men) around her. She's easily the most complex character in that particular film.

Yes, he is an 82-year-old-man with elements of the unreflected-upon male gaze that a lot of the male directors of his generation can frustratingly default to (and in that context he's if anything less egregious than many of his contemporaries) but I don't think his body of work is by any means absent of well and thoughtfully-realized women.
  - Came here to mention The New World as well.
  - Agreed, I think he’s comfortable exploring as a man in the world. The more ethereal female characters, like Jessica Chastain in ToL are, I think, intentionally so. Sean Penn is still searching to know his mother and father but they’re gone and they’re unknowable.


    - And in that film I think she in some sense stands in for “The Way of Grace” in contrast to the father who embodies “The Way of Nature.” So yes she is a real person, but we sees her in those early childhood memories as something ethereal, shimmering, and beyond because that’s how he remembers seeing her. 
4. I think just between Badlands and Days of Heaven we have our answer about how he has a balanced and considered approach to agency and depth in women. There is a whole world to consider in each of those characters and I think they feel very alive while in some ways still (realistic) archetypes of their era.

His later films are just generally all over the shop, so his portrayal of women is not one of the criticisms I feel a need to jump to.

## 2. Days of Heaven (1978) - Review

**Score:** 39
**Body:** I don't have words to describe what I felt while watching this one! My first time watching a Terrence Malick film. This was poetry in motion. The plot, the narrative - didn't even matter. This movie sucked me into it like no other. For 94 minutes straight, I felt like I was truly standing in America of the early 20th century next to these characters. I could smell the ripe wheat ready to be harvested. I could feel the breeze flowing across my face. I could feel the heat of the iron furnace and the open fire and the chaotic frenzy of the later. I have witnessed a locust swarm before and it was like I was taken back to that time again. I could feel the dread of a small creature that doesn't even attack humans - but the sheer number of them - eclipsing the sun itself is a very wild experience. 

Each shot was just perfection. This movie is as visually stunning as any movie ever made if not more. And then the heavenly score composed by Ennio Morricone and Leo Kottke...ufff! Even the narration by Linda was so soothing! 

It was so unique to me because it was the first time when I was able to let go of the plot completely and just absorb what I was seeing and hearing. Perhaps I will visit Tarkovsky again now!

Please also suggest which Malick film should I watch next!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. One of my favorites of all time! Glad you enjoyed it! It’s worth reading about the production, it was meant to be a completely different movie that changed a ton in the long editing processing and kind of led Malick to discover the type of filmmaker he is today where he spends years editing his movies and finding them. I think his movies are best watched basically in release order so go back and check out Badlands (his most straight forward narrative) and then go on to The Thin Red Line! And after that is The New World which you should seek out the extended cut of which is his preferred version. There’s also a longer cut for The Tree of Life that’s more supplementary for your 2nd viewing so watch the shorter cut of that one first. After that his filmography gets more polarizing with a trilogy of movies he basically shot at the same time, but there’s still so much to love if you love his style
  - Thank you for the reply! Looking forward to Badlands
2. Days of Heaven was my first Terence Malick movie and I was completely absorbed in its world. I knew nothing about Malick at the time and I have since gone through all of his movies, starting with Badlands and then moving onto The Thin Red Line before working my way through them in chronological order. I had initially hesitated checking out his later films because I heard some negative comments about them; however, I really enjoyed his weightless trilogy because they were just pure poetry to me. I stand by the belief that To the Wonder is his most underrated movie although my personal favourite has to be The Tree of Life (theatrical cut), a movie that is worth building toward after watching The Thin Red Line and The New World (extended cut)
3. I remember finding *Days of Heaven* in the '90s on what was then called American Movie Classics. This was long before they were the abbreviated network of *Mad Men* and *Breaking Bad*; they were still "older movies all the time," and 1978 was unusually recent for their programming.

It was the scene in which Linda's friend is running to catch the train, and she tells Linda to be good.

It wasn't that anything remarkable was happening on screen, but I was completely captivated by the look and sound of what I had channel-surfed onto: the sky and the grass, all the extras milling around on their own journeys, the sense that this intimate little moment between the girls was taking place against a vast backdrop, no more or less important than anything else...and of course, that Morricone score with its variations on the Saint-Saëns piece.

I remember looking to see when the next airing was, so I could see the whole movie. I don't really believe in having one favorite movie, but I've seen this one dozens of times and it comes as close as anything.
  - I saw it in ‘78, I was 8 years old. And I agree about how captivating this film is. It held my attention, I don’t recall feeling bored (like I did when I saw Reds, at age 11). The imagery was almost hypnotic. It says a lot for a film like that to entertain and hold the attention of an 8 year old kid. 
4. One of the most visually exquisite American films ever made, arguably Malick's *pièce de résistance* in that regard (major shoutout to Nestor Almendros for his cinematographic work here). The storyline is unsurprisingly thin, but as a mood piece/evocation of a place and time, I consider  it a standard-bearer. Also, Richard Gere gives what is perhaps the most unintentionally sexy performance of all time. 
5. [The Reflecting Skin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Reflecting_Skin) takes a similar palette and ratchets it up to 11. It's a fascinating movie, and one of the sunniest horror movies I've ever seen. The director even spray painted the wheat to make it glow more.
6. Got to see it in 35mm last year. Two days later, I couldn't stop thinking about it so I bought Criterion 4k. There really something about it that sucks you in. The story is pretty simple, but it stays with you.
7. I watched this last year and it was also my first Malick film. It's such an immersive experience with the beautiful cinematography and how they were able to incorporate so much farm machinery that it looks like a documentary from that time period.
8. Mesmerising  - with Malick, imo, Badlands, DOH, The Thin Red Line, Tree of Life and Knight of Cups are all essential. Badlands is really interesting as it helped inspire Bruce Springsteen's Nebraska album - he caught it on TV one night and it rocked him. Watch the film and then listen to the first track. Mallick got under the boss's skin, too...

A strange film that's always stuck in my mind as similar to DOH is Bill Forsyth's 1987 adaptation of Marilynne Robinson's Housekeeping. I haven't watched it since having this thought and might well be wrong, but it left me with some of the some awe (and sad wonder at what would become of these wonderful, poor characters)

## 3. The Secret Agent: The Crucial Sequence

**Score:** 39
**Body:** Man, what a seedy and brilliant film capturing of the tragic consequences of corruption, while also featuring one of the best sustained climaxes in recent memory. Although, I’m curious to hear if anyone had a similar experience watching the film, particularly the difference between the first 90 and final 90 minutes. 

Even though the initial chapters were interesting (the opening sequence sets the tone perfectly), I felt like from a narrative standpoint it kept it’s card fairly close to its chest — and I felt somewhat distant.

Although, the sequence involving the tapes and the flashbacks that establish the important expositional information regarding the relationship between Armando and Ghirotti were absolutely crucial for making completely invested. It really is a hack of human psychology in depicting an oppressive, inhumane force completely ruining the lives of innocent people that can’t help but make you feel a sense of retribution against unwarranted injustices. It was at this point that I couldn’t look away as the rest of the film was truly enthralling, capturing the tragic consequences of corruption and a subdued, but nevertheless, brutally tragic ending.

## 5. Fritz Lang’s The Big Heat (1953) and the banality of corruption

**Score:** 11
**Body:** Many crime films present corruption as something hidden in alleys or tucked away in some exotic, but generally separate criminal underworld. Fritz Lang’s The Big Heat uncovers it everywhere, and worse, it’s ordinary. The gangsters live comfortably, literally guarded by police officers and the politicians are bought and paid for. The violence happens right out in the open and is provided with a facade of respectability by our government and cultural institutions.

Glenn Ford’s Dave Bannion is not the cynic with a troubled past we typically see in film noir. He’s a genuinely honest man, who cares deeply about his family and his job. He has a wife he loves, a young child, a decent home, and a very stable sense of right and wrong. Bannion is basically an innocent man forced to look directly into the ugly moral center of his world, and the story becomes a test of whether that innocence can survive contact with power. Again and again, Lang pushes him toward murder, revenge, and moral collapse and again and again, Bannion gets right to the edge and stops himself.

But the really nasty twist of Bannion’s constant struggle is that the women around him pay the price for his ordeal. To me, Bannion is kind of an homme fatale. He doesn’t destroy women intentionally, but every woman who gets drawn into his orbit is marked for suffering or death. Lucy Chapman is tortured and murdered. His wife Katie is blown up in the car meant for him. Bertha Duncan dies because of the chain of events Bannion sets in motion. Debby is first scalded and then killed. The movie keeps asking Bannion to preserve his morality, forcing the women to bear the physical consequences.

Before Katie's death, Lang gives us a genuinely warm marriage, which is pretty rare in film noir. Katie and Bannion feel playful, intimate, alive together. Their home has real tenderness in it. So when she dies in that car bombing, the loss feels like the destruction of a whole moral framework. After that, all Bannion can do is try to exist in a world that has made his happiness look naive.

Gloria Grahame’s Debby starts out like a classic gangster’s moll, all surface and wisecracks and loungey glamour but Grahame plays her with such a peculiar mix of shallowness, hurt, and curiosity that Debby never feels one dimensional. She’s attracted to Bannion because he represents some kind of domestic life she’s never had. When Lee Marvin’s Vince Stone throws hot coffee in her face, it’s one of the most shocking scenes in noir, partly because it’s so sadistic and partly because it makes literal something the film has been hinting at all along. Debby has always been split between attraction to corruption and attraction to decency. After the attack, that inner division is literally written onto her body.

Marvin is terrifying as Stone, a sadist with social permission. He hurts women openly, casually, almost joyfully, and the system around him just absorbs it. The true horror of The Big Heat is that real life monsters function perfectly well inside respectable power structures. The police commissioner can comfortably sit nearby while a woman is mutilated. Everybody knows who runs the city and nobody meaningful intervenes.

In the end, Bannion wins, but in extremely narrow fashion. The corrupt machine takes a hit and the villains fall but the world isn't exactly restored as before. Bannion goes back to work. That’s it. No real healing, no emotional reunion, no fantasy that the nightmare has been cleansed. The system remains, and so does the need to keep fighting it. For me, that’s what makes The Big Heat so great. It takes the shape of a routine studio crime thriller and fills it with despair, moral pressure, showing how violence, power, and respectability all sustain one another.
